name: automate-whatsapp
description: Build WhatsApp automations with Kapso workflows: configure WhatsApp triggers, edit workflow graphs, manage executions, deploy functions, and use databases/integrations for state. Use when automating WhatsApp conversations and event handling.
capabilities: Create and manage WhatsApp workflow definitions with visual graph editing
Configure inbound message triggers for WhatsApp phone numbers
Deploy and invoke custom functions within workflow nodes
Query and manage D1 database tables for persistent state storage
Set up app integrations with external services via OAuth
Monitor execution history and debug workflow runs
author: sickn33
securitySummary: Static analysis flagged 137 patterns in SKILL.md documentation file, but all are false positives. The detected 'external_commands' are markdown code examples showing Node.js script usage, not actual shell execution. Network and env_access flags reference documentation content (GitHub URL and configuration examples), not runtime behavior. No malicious intent detected - this is legitimate workflow automation documentation for the Kapso platform.
valueStatement: Build sophisticated WhatsApp automations without manual coding. Configure workflow triggers, manage executions, and integrate with external services through the Kapso platform.
limitations: Requires valid Kapso platform account and API credentials
WhatsApp Business API approval needed for production messaging
Cannot execute raw SQL queries - limited to provided database operations
Variable management blocked - Platform API does not support variable CRUD operations

useCases.targetUser: Support team leads managing high-volume customer communications
useCases.title: Customer Support Automation
useCases.description: Build automated customer support flows that handle common inquiries, collect user information, and route complex cases to human agents via WhatsApp.

useCases.targetUser: E-commerce store owners using WhatsApp for customer updates
useCases.title: E-commerce Order Notifications
useCases.description: Create workflows that send order confirmations, shipping updates, and delivery notifications automatically when triggered by e-commerce platform events.

useCases.targetUser: Market researchers gathering customer insights via messaging
useCases.title: Interactive Survey Collection
useCases.description: Design interactive button-based surveys that collect customer feedback through WhatsApp with conditional branching based on responses.

promptTemplates.title: Create Simple Notification Workflow
promptTemplates.prompt: Create a WhatsApp workflow that sends a welcome message when a user first contacts the business. Include a start node and a message node with the welcome text.
promptTemplates.scenario: Beginner - Basic linear workflow with single message

promptTemplates.title: Build Decision-Based Routing
promptTemplates.prompt: Create a workflow with a decide node that routes users based on their message content. If message contains 'support', route to support team. If message contains 'sales', route to sales team. Otherwise, send a general inquiry response.
promptTemplates.scenario: Intermediate - Conditional branching based on message analysis

promptTemplates.title: Integrate External API for Data Lookup
promptTemplates.prompt: Build a workflow that looks up customer order status by phone number. Create a function node that queries the orders database, then send a personalized message with the order details to the customer.
promptTemplates.scenario: Advanced - Function integration with database queries

promptTemplates.title: Multi-Step Conversation with State Management
promptTemplates.prompt: Create an interactive survey workflow using button nodes. Collect user responses across multiple messages, store answers in execution context variables, and generate a summary at the end. Handle timeout scenarios if user stops responding.
promptTemplates.scenario: Expert - Stateful multi-turn conversation with timeout handling

bestPractices: Always fetch the workflow graph before editing to get the current lock_version and avoid conflicts
Use file paths for graph definitions and function code instead of inline JSON for better maintainability
Validate workflow graphs locally before submitting updates to catch structural errors early
Test trigger configurations in a development workflow before deploying to production phone numbers
Store sensitive credentials like API keys in environment variables, never in workflow definitions
antiPatterns: Changing existing node IDs in a workflow graph - this breaks execution history and references
Using export statements or arrow functions in function code - Kapso requires standard function handler syntax
Creating workflows with multiple start nodes - exactly one start node with id 'start' is required
Referencing variables without initializing them in the execution context first

faq.question: What do I need before using this skill?
faq.answer: You need a Kapso platform account, WhatsApp Business API access, and environment variables KAPSO_API_BASE_URL and KAPSO_API_KEY configured.

faq.question: Can I modify workflow graphs while they have active executions?
faq.answer: Yes, but use the lock_version mechanism. Fetch the graph first, make changes, then update with the expected lock_version to prevent conflicts.

faq.question: What programming languages work for custom functions?
faq.answer: Functions use JavaScript with a specific handler signature: async function handler(request, env). The code runs in Kapso's serverless runtime.

faq.question: How do I connect to external services like Slack or Google Sheets?
faq.answer: Use app integrations. Search for the app, get the action_id, create an integration with OAuth credentials, then reference it in agent nodes.

faq.question: Can I store data between workflow executions?
faq.answer: Yes, use D1 database tables for persistent storage or the KV store for simple key-value data accessible via env.KV and env.DB in functions.

faq.question: What happens if a workflow execution fails?
faq.answer: Failed executions can be inspected using get-execution and list-execution-events scripts. You can resume certain executions or force status updates for debugging.
